summary:
The Khmelnytsky Uprising (1648–1657 AD) was a massive [[event:3629|Cossack]] and peasant revolt against the [[event:2390|Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth]] led by Hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky — a conflict that simultaneously created an autonomous Ukrainian Cossack state, devastated the Commonwealth, and eventually aligned Ukraine with Russia through the Treaty of Pereyaslav (1654), with consequences that reverberate into the modern era. Khmelnytsky, a mid-ranking Cossack officer with personal grievances against a Polish noble, rallied the Zaporozhian Cossacks and allied with the Crimean Khan, defeating Polish armies in a series of devastating battles (Zhovti Vody, Korsun, Batih) and creating the Hetmanate — a Cossack-governed autonomous state in central Ukraine.

The uprising's violence was catastrophic, particularly for Ukraine's Jewish population: the Cossack forces massacred Jews in hundreds of towns, killing estimates ranging from 50,000 to 200,000 (roughly 20-25% of all Polish-Lithuanian Jews) — one of the worst pre-Holocaust Jewish disasters in history, commemorated as the Chmielnicki massacres. The uprising also killed hundreds of thousands of Polish and Ukrainian nobles, clergy, and peasants. Unable to defeat the Commonwealth alone without reliable allies (the Crimean Khan repeatedly abandoned him at crucial moments), Khmelnytsky turned to the Russian Tsar, leading to the Treaty of Pereyaslav (1654), which placed the Hetmanate under Russian sovereignty — a decision whose meaning was disputed from the start and whose consequences (was it union, protectorate, or vassalage?) have been contested by Ukrainian, Polish, and Russian historians ever since, with direct relevance to 21st-century disputes over Ukraine's sovereignty.
